coredata.dc:title: Development of a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform for acid phosphatase assay based on Fe3+ functionalized CuInS2/ZnS quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ruyi
Qi, Xiaofei
Wu, Fengyao
Liu, Cong
Huang, Xiaohua
Bai, Tianyu
Xing, Shanghua
coredata.dc:description:
Background The spectral dual-mode response towards analyte has been attracted much attention, benefiting from the higher detection accuracy of such strategy in comparison to single signal readout. However, the currently reported dual-mode sensors for acid phosphatase (ACP) activity are still limited, and most of them more or less exist some deficiencies, such as complicated construction procedure, high-cost, poor biocompatibility, aggregation-caused quenching and limited emission capacity.
Results Herein, we employed Fe3+ functionalized CuInS2/ZnS quantum dots (CIS/ZnS QDs) as nanosensor to develop a novel fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode assay for ACP activity, combing with ACP-triggered hydrolysis of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) into ascorbic acid (AA). The Fe3+ binding to CIS/ZnS QDs can be reduced into Fe2+ during the determination, resulting in the dramatically weakened photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect and the disappearance of competition absorption. Thus, a highly sensitive ACP assay in the range of 0.22–12.5 U L−1 through fluorescence “turn-on” mode has been achieved with a detection of limit (LOD) of 0.064 U L−1. Meanwhile, the ACP activity can also be quantified by spectrophotometry based on the chromogenic reaction of the formed Fe2+ with 1,10-phenanthroline (Phen). Moreover, the designed nanosensor with good biocompatibility was successfully applied to image and monitor the ACP levels in living cells.
Significance We believe that the proposed method has remarkable advantages and potential application for ACP assay in terms of the high accuracy, simplicity, low cost, as well as its adequate sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuInS2@Fe nanosensor
Quenching mechanism
Dual-mode assay
Acid phosphatase
Intracellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorometric and colorimetric assay of AA and ACP activity
Verification of the detection feasibility for ACP activity
Fluorometric and colorimetric detection of AA
Fluorometric and colorimetric assay for ACP activity
Imaging of ACP level in living cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescent titration spectra of CIS/ZnS QDs upon gradual addition of Fe3+ (0–180 μM). (b) Corresponding fluorescent intensity at 628 nm versus the concentration of Fe3+. Inset shows the linear calibration plot.

(a) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of CIS/ZnS QDs in the absence and presence of quencher (Fe3+, Fe2+) under laser excitation of 370 nm. Inset shows the table that lists the decay lifetime corresponding to each triexponential decays. (b) Scheme of the PET quenching mechanism of Fe3+ and Fe2+ to CIS/ZnS QDs. (c) The excitation spectrum of CIS/ZnS QDs, and the UV–vis absorption spectra Fe3+ and Fe2+. (d) Fluorescence spectra of CIS/ZnS QDs in the absence and presence of quencher (Fe3+, Fe2+) under excitation of 370 nm. All measurements were performed under 0.28 mg mL−1 CIS/ZnS QDs, 150 μM Fe3+ and Fe2+.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of individual CIS/ZnS@Fe, binary mixture of CIS/ZnS@Fe with AA (100 μM), ACP (12.5 U L−1), AAP (75 μM), and ternary mixture of CIS/ZnS@Fe, AAP (75 μM) with ACP (12.5 U L−1). Inset shows the corresponding photographs of the samples under excitation at 365 nm. (b) Uv–vis absorption spectra of individual CIS/ZnS@Fe, binary mixture of CIS/ZnS@Fe with Phen (600 μM), ternary mixture of CIS/ZnS@Fe, Phen with AA (100 μM), ACP (12.5 U L−1), AAP (75 μM), and quaternary mixture of CIS/ZnS@Fe, Phen, AAP (75 μM) with ACP (12.5 U L−1). Inset shows the corresponding photographs of the samples. All the measurements were performed in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 5.0).

(a) Fluorescent titration spectra of CIS/ZnS@Fe upon gradual addition of AA (0–200 μM). (b) Linear fitting curve between fluorescence intensity at 628 nm and concentration of AA from 0 to 125 μM. (c) Uv–vis titration spectra of [(Phen)3Fe]2+ complex produced in the response of CIS/ZnS@Fe towards AA at different concentration (0–150 μM). (d) Linear fitting curve between absorbance intensity at 510 nm and concentration of AA from 0 to 60 μM.

(a) Fluorescent titration spectra of the assay with various units of ACP activity (0–17.5 U L−1). (b) Linear fitting curve between fluorescence intensity at 628 nm and ACP level from 0 to 12.5 U L−1. (c) UV–vis titration spectra of assay with various units of ACP activity (0–20 U L−1). (d) Linear fitting curve between absorbance intensity at 510 nm and ACP level from 0 to 6 U L−1.

Fluorescence intensity at 628 nm (a), and absorbance intensity at 510 nm (b) of the assay in the absence (black column) and presence (red column) of ACP (12.5 U L−1) with coexistence (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, 1 mM; GOx, HRP, TRY, PAP, 62.5 U L−1; ALP, 12.5 U L−1; Gly, Glu, Trp, Asp, 100 μM; GSH, Cys, DTT, 50 μM). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescence images of Hela cells. From left to right: (a) CIS/ZnS QDs labeled cells; (b) CIS/ZnS@Fe labeled cells; (c) CIS/ZnS@Fe labeled cells incubated with AAP (75 μM); (d) Cells pre-treated with ACP inhibitor Na2MoO4 (100 μM), and then incubated with AAP (75 μM) after being labeled by CIS/ZnS@Fe; (e)–(f) CIS/ZnS@Fe labeled cells pre-took in exogenous 1 U L−1, 7 U L−1 and 15 U L−1 ACP, respectively, and then incubated with AAP (75 μM). All scale bars = 100 μm.
Schematic of fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode assay for ACP activity based on CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor.

head.ce:title: Development of a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform for acid phosphatase assay based on Fe3+ functionalized CuInS2/ZnS quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Medical Laboratory, Dalian Medical University, No. 9 West Section Lvshun South Road, Dalian, 116044, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Food Interdisciplinary Science, School of Food Science and Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, 116034, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Food Interdisciplinary Science, School of Food Science and Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian 116034, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The spectral dual-mode response towards analyte has been attracted much attention, benefiting from the higher detection accuracy of such strategy in comparison to single signal readout. However, the currently reported dual-mode sensors for acid phosphatase (ACP) activity are still limited, and most of them more or less exist some deficiencies, such as complicated construction procedure, high-cost, poor biocompatibility, aggregation-caused quenching and limited emission capacity.

Herein, we employed Fe3+ functionalized CuInS2/ZnS quantum dots (CIS/ZnS QDs) as nanosensor to develop a novel fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode assay for ACP activity, combing with ACP-triggered hydrolysis of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) into ascorbic acid (AA). The Fe3+ binding to CIS/ZnS QDs can be reduced into Fe2+ during the determination, resulting in the dramatically weakened photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect and the disappearance of competition absorption. Thus, a highly sensitive ACP assay in the range of 0.22–12.5 U L−1 through fluorescence “turn-on” mode has been achieved with a detection of limit (LOD) of 0.064 U L−1. Meanwhile, the ACP activity can also be quantified by spectrophotometry based on the chromogenic reaction of the formed Fe2+ with 1,10-phenanthroline (Phen). Moreover, the designed nanosensor with good biocompatibility was successfully applied to image and monitor the ACP levels in living cells.
We believe that the proposed method has remarkable advantages and potential application for ACP assay in terms of the high accuracy, simplicity, low cost, as well as its adequate sensitivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acid phosphatase (ACP), mainly generated in lysosome and widely distributed in mammalian tissues and liquids, exercises catalytic function in the dephosphorylation of various monophosphate monoesters under weak acidic environment, and plays an essential effect on the regulation of various physiological processes [1–3]. Meanwhile, ACP can also serve as biomarker and prognostic indicator since its abnormal activity level is associated with several diseases, including prostate cancer, thrombophlebitis, and Gaucher's disease etc [4,5]. Thus, the accurate monitoring of ACP activity is of great significance in pathologic diagnosis, postsurgical evaluation and drug screening.

Recently, it has been the object of interest to develop the sensing platform with fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode response towards biomolecular [6–9]. Compared with single modal spectral response, it is recognized that dual-mode detection methods have the advantages as follows: (i) give more accurate analysis results attributing to the mutual correction between two-channel signals, which can overcome the susceptibility of single-mode method to the complex biological environment that resulting in unreliable quantitative bioassays; (ii) integrate the benefits of both, including the simplicity and rapidity for colorimetric assay and the superior temporal-spatial resolution in visualization of biomolecular for fluorometric assay; (iii) improve the detective performance, reflecting in the expansion of linear response range of signal towards analyte or the lowering of detection of limit (LOD). Drove by abovementioned superiorities, there have been reported several fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensors for ACP assay [10–13]. For example, Ran et al. constructed a colorimetric and fluorometric dual-channel sensor for ACP assay based on enzyme activity triggered blocking of internal filtration effect between rhodamine (RB) and oxidized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (oxTMB), and corresponding quantitative range was 0.06–5.0 mU mL−1 and 0.03–5.0 mU mL−1, respectively [11]. Liu et al. developed a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode platform for ACP activity through integrating stimulus-responsive luminescence with oxidase-mimetic activity into cerium-based coordination polymer nanoparticles (CPNs(IV)), and this platform can be further extended to the detection of pesticides [13]. It can be found that most of the developed methods employed organic dyes, noble metal nanoparticles and conjugated polymers, while all may more or less suffer from complicated construction procedure, high-cost, poor biocompatibility, aggregation-caused quenching and limited emission capacity, restricting their widespread application [14,15]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop and enrich more fluorometric and colorimetric dual-signal sensing platforms with high efficiency for ACP assay.

I-III-VI quantum dots (QDs) take the advantages of their excellent optical performance, moderate reaction condition, low toxicity and easy post-functional modification [16,17]. However, most of their applications in biosensing, including ACP assay, are limited to single-mode spectral readout and there are few reports extending such materials to fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode detection system [18–20]. It is worth noting that ferric ion (Fe3+) is both good fluorescence quencher and oxidizing agent under acidic condition, and thus usually used as recognition group to participate in the construction of “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for the detection of some antioxidant agents, such as ascorbic acid (AA) or some hydrolases relating to AA-generation or consumption [21,22]. Simultaneously, as the reduction product of Fe3+, the amount of formed ferrous ion (Fe2+) also relies on the target analyte level, which can be quantified by spectrophotometry based on chromogenic reaction. It inspires us to realize that Fe3+ has the potential to bridge the colorimetric signal readout to the I-III-VI QDs fluorescent switch, achieving fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode assay for ACP activity as shown in Scheme 1 .

Herein, we employed Fe3+ functionalized CuInS2/ZnS QDs (CIS/ZnS@Fe) as the nanosensor to develop a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform for ACP assay. Beforehand, the sensing principle of the developed platform was systematically verified to assess the feasibility of the developed strategy, especially for the confirmation and elucidation for the dramatic difference in fluorescence quenching efficiency between Fe3+ and Fe2+ to CIS/ZnS QDs. On the basis, the sensing performance of the CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor towards AA and further ACP activity were carried out under the optimal reactive condition, including the reaction kinetics, sensitivity and selectivity. Moreover, we investigated the potential application of the constructed CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor in monitoring the activity change of intracellular ACP through bioimaging.

The CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor was prepared by simply mixing the CIS/ZnS QDs and FeCl3 solution. In detail, 160 μL of CIS/ZnS QDs solution (3.5 mg mL−1) and 40 μL of FeCl3 (7.5 mM) solution were evenly mixed in 1.8 mL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 5.0, 50 mM). After shaking for 10 min, the CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor with concentration of 0.28 mg mL−1 for CIS/ZnS component and 150 μM for Fe3+ component was obtained, and then used for AA and ACP assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric and colorimetric assay of AA and ACP activity
ce:section.ce:para: For fluorometric quantitative determination of AA, varying amounts of AA were added to CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor. Then, the mixture was incubated for 40 min at room temperature. For fluorometric assay of ACP activity, varying units of ACP activity were mixed with 40 μL of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP, 3.75 mM), and then diluted to 200 μL with Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 5.0, 50 mM). The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. Then, the resulting solution was added to the CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor, and incubated for another 40 min at room temperature. The emission spectra (480–720 nm) of all the reaction solutions were recorded under 370 nm excitation by fluorescence spectrometer.

The method for the colorimetric assay of AA and ACP activity was similar to above fluorometric assay, except that 600 μM of 1,10-phenanthroline (Phen) was added into the reactive system after incubating the analyte with CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor for 40 min. The absorption spectra of the reaction solutions in the range of 300–800 nm were recorded by UV–vis spectrometer.

The Hela cells were divided into seven groups and treated differently for the subsequent imaging, respectively. All the solutes, including CIS/ZnS QDs, Fe3+, AAP, ACP and Na2MoO4, were dissolved into Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 5.0, 50 mM), and the obtained solutions were used in the following incubation with cells.

Typically, HeLa cells were first seeded into a 24-well plate and cultured overnight in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) in an incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then, the medium was removed and the adherent cells were washed three times with PBS buffer solution.

Next, Group 1 was incubated with CIS/ZnS QDs (0.28 mg mL−1) at 37 °C for 1 h to obtain the CIS/ZnS QDs labeled cells; Group 2 incubated with CIS/ZnS QDs (0.28 mg mL−1) at 37 °C for 1 h, followed by treating with Fe3+ (150 μM) for 10 min after washing with PBS buffer solution three times to obtain CIS/ZnS@Fe labeled cells; Group 3 was further incubated with AAP (75 μM) at 37 °C for 40 min after being labeled by CIS/ZnS@Fe; Group 4 was first incubated with ACP inhibitor Na2MoO4 (100 μM) at 37 °C for 50 min, and the following procedures were the same with that of Group 3; Group 5–7 were incubated with exogenous ACP at concentration of 1, 7, and 15 U L−1 respectively at 37 °C for 50 min after being labeled by CIS/ZnS@Fe, and then cultured with AAP (75 μM) at 37 °C for 40 min.

Finally, all the free substances were removed by washing the cells with PBS buffer solution for three times, and then the bright field and fluorescent images of Hela cells was recorded on inverted fluorescence microscope.
The reagents, experimental details of CIS/ZnS QDs preparation, cell viability assay, as well as the characterizations can be found in the Supporting Information.

The synthesis of CIS/ZnS QDs with glutathione (GSH) as surfactant referred to An's report with slight modifications [23]. Briefly, metal chloride precursors were dissolved into deionized water, and reacted with Na2S·9H2O to yield CIS core at 100 °C for 30 min. Then, shell precursors were added to above reactive system under the same temperature, followed by the epitaxial growth for 20 min to yield CIS/ZnS QDs. As shown in Fig. S1a, the XRD pattern of as-synthesized CIS/ZnS QDs exhibits a narrower full width at half-maximum (FWHM) than that of CIS core, and both of which are all agreement with the bulk CuInS2 chalcopyrite phase (JCPDS, no. 85–1575). The result indicates the successful overcoating of ZnS shell on the surface of CIS core, leading to the larger particle size for CIS/ZnS QDs. The TEM and HRTEM images (Fig. S1b) show that the CIS/ZnS QDs present both good dispersity without agglomeration and high crystallinity with well-ordered lattice fringes, and their average size is 3.12 ± 0.3 nm which is consistent with the value (∼2.65 nm) estimated by Scherrer equation. XPS surface analysis (Fig. S2) confirms the valences states of Cu+ (Cu 2p3/2 931.8 eV; Cu 2p1/2, 951.6 eV, peak separation of 19.8 eV), In3+ (In 3d5/2, 444.4 eV; In 3d3/2, 451.9 eV, peak separation of 7.5 eV), S2− (S 2p3/2, 161.15eV; S 2p1/2, 162.15 eV, peak separation of 1.0 eV), and Zn2+ (Zn 2p3/2, 1021.4 eV; Zn 2p1/2, 1044.3 eV, peak separation of 22.9 eV) [23,24]. In addition, no satellite peak around 942.6 eV appears, suggesting the inexistence of Cu2+ in CIS/ZnS QDs. In addition, the FT-IR spectrum of CIS/ZnS QDs (Fig. S3) shows the disappearance of absorption band at 2525 cm−1 (stretching vibration of –SH in free GSH), indicating the binding of GSH to the surface of CIS/ZnS QDs through –SH group [23,25].

The UV–vis absorption and luminescence emission properties of CIS/ZnS QDs were also characterized as shown in Fig. S4. Compared with CIS core, CIS/ZnS QDs present the slight blue shift of absorption edge, broadened bandgap energy (E g) and significantly enhanced emission intensity. It further confirms that the ZnS shell is coated on the surface of CIS core accompanying with partial Zn2+ diffusion into CIS core lattice (also leading to a slight shift towards higher 2θ angle for the XRD pattern of CIS/ZnS QDs than CIS core as shown in Fig. S1a), resulting in the effective inhibition of nonradiative recombination caused by the surface vacancies and structural defects for CIS core [17,26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the detection feasibility for ACP activity
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ usually uses as an excellent quencher to construct the fluorescence switch for biosensing [21,22,27,28]. As shown in Fig. 1 , the fluorescence intensity of CIS/ZnS QDs presents a gradual decline until imperceptibility with the increased amount of Fe3+, suggesting the effective binding of Fe3+ on the CIS/ZnS QDs to form CIS/ZnS@Fe complex. Notably, the CIS/ZnS QDs also present the potential for the quantitative detection of Fe3+ with LOD of ∼0.66 μM (within in the maximum permissible level of 5.4 μM for Fe3+) according to a linear regression curve obtained from the normalized fluorescence intensity versus log [Fe3+] (Fig. 1b, inset) [29,30]. Since the fluorescence intensity of CIS/ZnS QDs reaches the minimum when the concentration of Fe3+ is up to 150 μM, whose value was selected to construct CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor for the following quantitative analysis for AA and ACP.

Moreover, the difference of fluorescence quenching efficiency between Fe3+ and Fe2+ to CIS/ZnS QDs was investigated, whose extent is essential to the performance of our sensing platform. At first, time resolved photoluminescence spectra was performed for the direct insight of their exact quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the lifetime decay curves are well fitted with triexponential equation for the CIS/ZnS QDs without and with 150 μM quencher (either Fe3+ or Fe2+). It can be found that both Fe3+ and Fe2+ can cause the decay times τ 1, τ 2, τ 3 and the resulted average lifetime of the CIS/ZnS QDs to decrease (Fig. 2a, inset), implying their dynamic quenching process probably owing to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [21,30]. In order to verify this mechanism, we estimated the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) of CIS/ZnS QDs based on the empirical formulas shown in Supporting Information [31,32], whose value were ∼1.408 eV and −0.812 eV. Clearly, the reduction potentials for Fe3+/Fe2+ (0.771 eV) and Fe2+/Fe (−0.447 eV) all locate between the VB and CB of CIS/ZnS QDs [33]. It means that the electrons photoexcited to CB would transfer to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ and Fe2+, quenching the fluorescence of CIS/ZnS QDs through PET as shown in Fig. 2b. Usually, in PET, a charge transfer complex would be formed between electron donor and corresponding acceptor upon excitation [34]. It is noted that Linehan et al. proposed that Fe3+ is in favor of fluorescence quenching in thermodynamics compared with Fe2+ in this case because of more negative contribution of Fe3+/Fe2+ to the overall free energy change for this electron transfer reaction [35]. Considering the same quenching process, we judged their mechanism explanation was also applicable to our system. This deduction can be supported by the result that the addition of Fe3+ caused more significant attenuation for average lifetime, suggesting the enhanced PET effect [36]. On the other hand, the excitation spectrum of CIS/ZnS QDs is partial overlap with the absorption spectrum of Fe3+, while it is completely separated with that of Fe2+ as shown in Fig. 2c. The competition absorption upon the excitation (370 nm) between Fe3+ and CIS/ZnS QDs can further strength the quenching behavior of Fe3+ on CIS/ZnS QDs [37]. As expected, Fig. 2d shows that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of 150 μM Fe3+ to CIS/ZnS QDs (∼97.5%) was significantly superior than that of 150 μM Fe2+ (∼36.1%) alternative.

On the basis, the feasibility of CIS/ZnS@Fe as a fluorescence sensor for ACP assay was assessed as shown in Fig. 3 a. It can be found that both the fluorescence intensity and shape of the CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor exhibited obvious recovery in the presence of AA (red line) or the mixture of AAP and ACP (pink line), whereas the intensity still maintained much lower in the presence of substrate AAP (navy line) or individual ACP (blue line). This result confirms that ACP hydrolyzed AAP to form AA which then reacted with Fe3+ to switch on the fluorescence signal.

Subsequently, Phen was used to further verify the response principle since it can specifically recognize Fe2+ to form orange [(Phen)3Fe]2+ complex [38,39]. As shown in Fig. 3b, a weak absorption peak appeared at ∼510 nm after adding Phen into the CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor (orange line), indicating the existence of a handful of Fe2+, which may be attributed to the reduction of Fe3+ by the free GSH existed in the system or by the exposure of outermost Cu+ in CIS crystal lattice. But the addition of individual AAP (navy line) or ACP (blue line) hardly affect the absorption spectrum of Phen-CIS/ZnS@Fe system, suggesting the termination in conversion from Fe3+ to Fe2+. By contrast, the addition of AA (red line) or the mixture of ACP and AAP (pink line) can lead to a typical absorption spectrum of [(Phen)3Fe]2+ complex, confirming the above fluorescence response being attributed to different quenching efficiency between Fe3+ and Fe2+. More importantly, it suggests that the ACP activity can also be quantified by spectrophotometry based on the chromogenic reaction of the formed Fe2+ with Phen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric and colorimetric detection of AA
ce:section.ce:para: Besides being an excellent antioxidant, AA can also act as a transmission signal in the assay of several bio-enzymes activity [40], and thus was detected quantitatively based on the CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor. For fluorescence experiments, the condition optimization, including pH and reactive time was carried out firstly. Fig. S5 shows the fluorescence intensity ratio at ∼628 nm for CIS/ZnS@Fe in the presence and absence of AA (100 μM) as the pH values range from 3.0 to 11.0, whose maximum locates at pH 5.0. It is because that Fe3+ usually exhibits oxidability under acidic conditions, while its binding ability to CIS/ZnS QDs will be extremely limited in strong acidic medium resulting from the surface charge change caused by the protonation of CIS/ZnS QDs. Notably, pH 5.0 as the optimal condition was not only commonly reported for ACP assay but also for chromogenic reaction with Phen [11,15,41,42], which was adopted in the subsequent experiments. Fig. S6 shows that the fluorescence intensity of CIS/ZnS@Fe at 628 nm increased gradually until reaching a plateau at ∼40 min upon the addition of AA (100 μM), and the pseudo first-order rate constant (k obs) was calculated to be 0.0678 min−1 (inset of Fig. S6b). Thus, the reactive time was set to be 40 min for the subsequent quantitative determination.

Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence sensing performance of CIS/ZnS@Fe to AA was evaluated. Fig. 4 a shows a continuous fluorescence recovery as the concentration of AA increasing from 0 to 150 μM. The relationship between the fluorescence peak intensity at 628 nm and the concentration of AA in the range of 0.28–125 μM exhibited good linearity, and the correlation equation is I = 9.325C - 22.237 (R 2 = 0.9978) as shown in Fig. 4b. Accordingly, the LOD was determined to be 0.083 μM (S/N = 3).

For colorimetric sensing of AA, the absorption intensity of CIS/ZnS@Fe at 510 nm also exhibited continuous increment, and good linear relationship with the concentration of AA in the range of 2.63–60 μM. The correlation equation was A = 0.0136C + 0.661 (R 2 = 0.9937) as shown in Fig. 4c–d. Accordingly, the LOD was determined to be 0.79 μM. The sensing performance of colorimetric mode for our developed platform was not as good as that of fluorometric mode, which can be attributed to the inherent brownish of CIS/ZnS and partial conversion of Fe3+ to Fe2+ on CIS/ZnS@Fe before sensing. Nevertheless, above results still confirm the potential to quantitatively determinate AA as well as the compounds involving AA-generation or consumption through fluorometry and colorimetry based on CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric and colorimetric assay for ACP activity
ce:section.ce:para: Benefiting from AA-triggered dual signal readout, the quantitative assay of ACP activity was performed with the assistant of substrate AAP. In order to maximize the determination performance, the effect of pH in the range of 3.0–11.0 and the incubation time was first investigated for the hydrolysis of AAP by ACP. As shown in Fig. S7, it can be found that the fluorescence intensity at 628 nm increased to the maximum at pH 5.0 and reach an equilibrium point in 40 min, both of which were selected in the subsequent experiments. With ascertained experimental conditions, Fig. 5 a–b shows a continuously enhanced fluorescence spectra as the increased ACP activity, and reaches plateau at 12.5 U L−1. Fig. 5b shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity at 628 nm and the ACP activity range of 0.22–12.5 U L−1. The correlation equation is expressed as I = 27.473C + 60.461 (R 2 = 0.9993) with the determined LOD of 0.064 U L−1.

On the other hand, the colorimetric assay of ACP activity was also evaluated by adding Phen into the system after reacting sufficiently between CIS/ZnS@Fe and enzymatic product. As shown in Fig. 5c–d, the absorption intensity at 510 nm was enhanced linearly upon the ACP activity over the range of 0.58–6.0 U L−1, suggesting that the concentration of the formed [(Phen)3Fe]2+ complex highly relies on ACP activity. The correlation equation is expressed as A = 0.0800C + 0.666 (R 2 = 0.9824) with the determined LOD of 0.175 U L−1.

In comparison with previously reported probes, the proposed CIS/ZnS@Fe all exhibited roughly comparable performance on the aspects of fluorometric and colorimetric mode (Table S1). As a result, our developed platform is successful to integrate fluorometric and colorimetric dual readout signal and take both LOD and linear range into account, implying its high reliability, precision and sensitivity for ACP assay. In addition, the relative standard deviation (RSD) calculated from fluorescence measurements of 6 U L−1 ACP and UV–vis measurements of 3 U L−1 ACP in five replicates was ±2.82% and ±1.78% respectively, indicating the good reproducibility of our platform [43–45].

In the actual bio-application, it is essential for the designed platform to possess high recognition towards ACP over other interferences. To examine the selectivity of the developed platform for ACP assay, the effect of several potential coexisting substances, including ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Ma2+), common enzymes (GOx, HRP, ALP, Try, PAP, five times higher than ACP activity), small biomolecules (Gly, Glu, Trp, Asp) and biothiols (GSH, Cys, DTT) were tested for the fluorometric and colorimetric assay of 12.5 U L−1 ACP. Although some reductive species (e.g., biothiols, Asp) can trigger slight chromogenic reaction, the fluorescence intensity at 628 nm and absorption at 510 nm of the detection system remained nearly constant in the presence of most interfering substances (black column), and all increased closely to the level in the case of without interfering substances after ACP assay (red column) as shown in Fig. 6 . These results confirm the outstanding anti-interference and selectivity ability of the developed platform for ACP assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of ACP level in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: The satisfactory results in in vitro experiments encouraged us to evaluate the potential of the CIS/ZnS@Fe for intracellular ACP imaging. Prior to cell imaging, we carried out CCK-8 assay to specially verify the cytotoxicity of CIS/ZnS QDs considering the well-known functioning in biology for Fe3+. As shown in Fig. S8, the cell viability all can reach more than 80% after incubating the Hela cells with different concentrations of CIS/ZnS QDs (0.07–0.35 mg mL−1) together for 24 h, implying the good biocompatibility of the detection platform constructed by CIS/ZnS QDs.

On the basis, we investigated the ability to label cell for CIS/ZnS@Fe. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the strong red fluorescence signal emitting from the Hela cells confirmed that the CIS/ZnS QDs can free access to the cytoplasm by permeation benefiting from its ultrasmall size. The red fluorescence signal can be significantly quenched followed by further incubating with Fe3+ (Fig. 7b) for 10 min, indicating the successful formation of CIS/ZnS@Fe in Hela cells. In addition, there were no fluorescent recovery for the CIS/ZnS@Fe labeled cells when they were further incubated with Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 5.0) for 50 and 100 min as shown in Fig. S9. It should be noted that the CIS/ZnS@Fe labeled cells were obtained by post-treating these CIS/ZnS labeled cells with Fe3+ rather than the co-incubation of Hela cells with pre-prepared CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor. In this case, we consider that the Fe3+ can offset the reductive species in cells during its penetration into the cytoplasm, and thus eliminate the interference of the reductive species as much as possible in the following ACP bio-imaging. Subsequently, the endogenous ACP fluorescence imaging in these CIS/ZnS@Fe labeled Hela cells were conducted owing to the overexpression of ACP in Hela cells [44,46]. Compared with the just CIS/ZnS@Fe labeled Hela cells, a recognizable fluorescence signal was displayed from the cells after further incubating them with AAP (Fig. 7c). This observation demonstrated the capacity of CIS/ZnS@Fe to image the endogenous ACP activity through the hydrolysis of AAP by ACP.

Further, we investigated the ability of the CIS/ZnS@Fe to monitor the activity change of intracellular ACP through treating the cells with ACP inhibitor (Na2MoO4, 100 μM) or exogenous ACP. As shown in Fig. 7d, the red fluorescence signal exhibited indistinct, suggesting the weakened endogenous ACP activity caused by the effective inhibition of Na2MoO4. By comparison, the intracellular fluorescence signal exhibited gradual increment upon the exposure of Hela cells to the increased ACP activity (Fig. 7e–g). These observations clearly suggest that the CIS/ZnS@Fe possesses the potential to be a sensitive sensor for specifically endogenous and exogenous ACP imaging in living cells. In addition, the fluorescence intensities of intracellular ACP imaging were analyzed by software Image J, which present a linear relationship with the standard addition amount of ACP in the range of 0–7 U L−1 as shown in Fig. S10, implying the possibility of application in quantification of ACP levels in vivo for the CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor.

In summary, we have successfully constructed a CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor based sensing platform in fluorometric and colorimetric response to AA, and further developed it for ACP assay. In comparation to Fe2+, Fe3+ could quench the fluorescence intensity of CIS/ZnS QDs much more efficiently due to its stronger PET effect and competition absorption with the fluorophore, thereby endowing the CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor with enhancement of fluorescent signal in the presence of AA. Simultaneously, the AA-induced formation of Fe2+ also enables the CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor to output colorimetric signal through the chromogenic reaction with Phen. With such a principle, the developed platform can provide dual-mode signals for ACP assay by the ACP-triggered hydrolysis of AAP to AA, showing the advantages of reliability of detection results, simplicity of operation, and high sensitivity and selectivity. Furthermore, the CIS/ZnS@Fe nanosensor exhibited good biocompatibility, and applied as a fluorescent probe to monitor the activity variation of ACP in living cells. More in-depth research for our developed dual-signal sensing system will focus on expanding its application field, such as tumor marker bioanalysis or enzyme inhibitor screening.

Ruyi Li: Data curation, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Xiaofei Qi: Data curation, Formal analysis. Fengyao Wu: Validation. Cong Liu: Data curation. Xiaohua Huang: Funding acquisition. Tianyu Bai: Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Shanghua Xing: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Foundation of National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21701020), the Foundation of Liaoning Province Education Administration (no. LJKMZ20221262).
